From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Institution that provides financial services for its clients or members

"Banking institutions" redirects here. For banks as financial institutions, see [Bank](/wiki/Bank "Bank").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena_in_Pisa.JPG/250px-Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena_in_Pisa.JPG)](/wiki/File:Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena_in_Pisa.JPG)

The [oldest financial institution](/wiki/List_of_oldest_banks_in_continuous_operation "List of oldest banks in continuous operation") in the world, [Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena](/wiki/Banca_Monte_dei_Paschi_di_Siena "Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena"), founded in 1472.

A **financial institution**, sometimes called a **banking institution**, is a [business entity](/wiki/Business_entity "Business entity") that provides service as an intermediary for different types of financial monetary transactions.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_institution&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

Broadly speaking, there are three major types of financial institution:[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

1. Depository institution – [deposit](/wiki/Deposit_(finance) "Deposit (finance)")-taking institution that accepts and manages deposits and makes [loans](/wiki/Loan "Loan"), including [bank](/wiki/Bank "Bank"), [building society](/wiki/Building_society "Building society"), [credit union](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union"), [trust company](/wiki/Trust_company "Trust company"), and [mortgage broker](/wiki/Mortgage_broker "Mortgage broker");
2. Contractual institution – [insurance company](/wiki/Insurance_company "Insurance company") and [pension fund](/wiki/Pension_fund "Pension fund")
3. Investment institution – [investment bank](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking"), [underwriter](/wiki/Underwriter "Underwriter"), and other different types of financial entities managing investments.

Financial institutions can be distinguished broadly into two categories according to ownership structure:

* [commercial bank](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank")
* [cooperative bank](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking")

Some experts see a trend toward [homogenisation](/wiki/Homogenisation "Homogenisation") of financial institutions, meaning a tendency to invest in similar areas and have similar business strategies. A consequence of this might be fewer banks serving specific target groups, and small-scale producers may be under-served.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This is why a target of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 10 is to improve the regulation and monitoring of global financial institutions and strengthen such regulations.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Standard settlement instructions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_institution&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Standard settlement instructions")]

Standard Settlement Instructions (SSIs) are the agreements between two financial institutions which fix the receiving agents of each [counterparty](/wiki/Counterparty "Counterparty") in ordinary trades of some type. These agreements allow the related [counterparties](/wiki/Counterparty "Counterparty") to make faster operations since the time used to settle the receiving agents is conserved. Limiting each subject to an SSI also lowers the likelihood of a [fraud](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud"). SSIs are used by financial institutions to facilitate fast and accurate cross-border payments.

## Regulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_institution&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Regulation")]

Financial institutions in most countries operate in a heavily regulated environment because they are critical parts of countries' economies, due to economies' dependence on them to grow the money supply via [fractional-reserve banking](/wiki/Fractional-reserve_banking "Fractional-reserve banking"). Regulatory structures differ in each country, but typically involve prudential regulation as well as [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection") and market stability. Some countries have one consolidated agency that regulates all financial institutions while others have separate agencies for different types of institutions such as banks, insurance companies and brokers.

Countries that have separate agencies include the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where the key governing bodies are the [Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council](/wiki/Federal_Financial_Institutions_Examination_Council "Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council") (FFIEC), [Office of the Comptroller of the Currency](/wiki/Office_of_the_Comptroller_of_the_Currency "Office of the Comptroller of the Currency") – National Banks, [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") (FDIC) State "non-member" banks, [National Credit Union Administration](/wiki/National_Credit_Union_Administration "National Credit Union Administration") (NCUA) – Credit Unions, [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System") (Fed) – "member" banks, [Office of Thrift Supervision](/wiki/Office_of_Thrift_Supervision "Office of Thrift Supervision") – National Savings & Loan Association, [State governments](/wiki/State_government "State government") each often regulate and charter financial institutions.

Countries that have one consolidated financial regulator include: Norway with the [Financial Supervisory Authority of Norway](/wiki/Financial_Supervisory_Authority_of_Norway "Financial Supervisory Authority of Norway"), Germany with [Federal Financial Supervisory Authority](/wiki/Federal_Financial_Supervisory_Authority "Federal Financial Supervisory Authority") and Russia with [Central Bank of Russia](/wiki/Central_Bank_of_Russia "Central Bank of Russia").

## Merits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_institution&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Merits")]

Merits of raising funds through financial institutions are as follows:

1. Financial institutions provide [long term finance](http://www.worldbank.org/en/publication/gfdr/gfdr-2016/background/long-term-finance), which are not provided by [commercial banks](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank");
2. The funds are made available even during periods of depression, when other sources of finance are not available;
3. Obtaining loan from financial institutions increases the goodwill of the borrowing in the [capital market](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market"). Consequently, such a company can raise funds easily from other sources as well;
4. Besides providing funds, many of these institutions provide financial, managerial and technical advice and consultancy to business firms;
5. As repayment of loan can be made in easy installments, it does not prove to be much of burden on the business.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Financial_institution&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Ethical banking](/wiki/Ethical_banking "Ethical banking")

* [International financial institutions](/wiki/International_financial_institutions "International financial institutions")

* [Non-bank financial institution](/wiki/Non-bank_financial_institution "Non-bank financial institution")
* [Savings and loan association](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association")
* [Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication](/wiki/Society_for_Worldwide_Interbank_Financial_Telecommunication "Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication")
